narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. As a male Asian student from a middle-income family planning to study full-time, you have a profile with several strengths like being in the majority for full-time enrollment, but your academic history stood out as the main concern. Compared to typical students—who average around the 5th or 6th decile for Year 1 and Year 3 school rankings, an LSAT score near 37, and an undergrad GPA over 3.2—your Year 3 decile rank of 2 was in the bottom tier and had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 32 nudged the prediction further in the same direction, more than your Year 1 decile of 2 did. Your middle family income and Asian racial background each played smaller but still notable roles in tipping the scales toward failure, followed by your undergrad GPA of 2.4, full-time status, and gender, which had the least influence overall. In essence, every aspect of your profile contributed somewhat to the failure prediction, but your later-year school performance and standardized test score mattered most because they signal potential challenges in mastering the intense legal material needed for the bar. This ranking shows how the model weighed your lower-than-average academics as the dominant factors, unlike many admitted students whose stronger records push predictions toward passing. While demographics like race and income influenced the outcome less, they still factored in based on patterns from past bar-takers. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT to aim higher than 37, build a stronger academic record through postgraduate courses or targeted prep to boost your effective GPA equivalent, and seek bar-specific study strategies early, as your school deciles suggest room for better performance habits. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on. You're close to the edge at 51%, so even modest improvements could flip the prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to make this happen.
